1: YHWH makes Moses 'elohim to Pharaoh' — a god-like figure before the earthly ruler. The word elohim here assigns Moses a divine representative role: he speaks for YHWH to the ruler of the most powerful empire on earth. This prefigures Christ's role as the definitive Word of God confronting the 'ruler of this world' (John 12:31). As Moses is YHWH's elohim-figure to Pharaoh, so Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' (Colossians 1:15) who confronts and defeats every power that claims sovereignty over humanity.
2: Moses speaks everything YHWH commands; Aaron speaks to Pharaoh. The mediatorial chain — YHWH → Moses → Aaron → Pharaoh — anticipates the NT pattern where the divine word passes through the Son to the world. Jesus is both Moses and Aaron in one: he is both the one who receives the Father's word perfectly (John 8:28) and the one who speaks it directly to the world without an intermediary. The doubled mediation of the exodus gives way to the single perfect Mediator (1 Timothy 2:5).
3: 'I will harden Pharaoh's heart (ani aksheh et-lev par'oh).' The divine hardening is already announced before the plague sequence begins. Theologically, this anticipates Paul's treatment of divine sovereignty and human responsibility in Romans 9:17–18, where he quotes Exodus 9:16 and cites Pharaoh as the paradigmatic case of divine governance over the obstinate. The hardened heart is the condition of those who resist the word of God persistently — a condition that only Christ's new-covenant work reverses (Ezekiel 36:26; 2 Corinthians 3:14–16).
4: 'Egypt will not listen to you' — the refusal of the word is announced in advance. This matches the Johannine prologue pattern: 'He came to his own and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). Jesus taught in parables partially because of the same dynamic — the word spoken to a hardened audience increases their accountability without converting them (Matthew 13:13–15, quoting Isa 6). The exodus refuses and the ministry refuses are structurally parallel: a nation's rejection of YHWH's messenger leads to judgment.
5: 'The Egyptians shall know that I am YHWH when I stretch out my hand over Egypt.' The goal of the plagues is revelatory — YHWH's identity as sovereign Lord is to be known through the judgment. This knowledge-through-judgment formula anticipates the cross: it was through the seeming defeat of crucifixion that 'all peoples' would be drawn to acknowledge the crucified one (John 12:32; Philippians 2:9–11). Both at the Red Sea and at Golgotha, YHWH's identity as Lord is revealed precisely in the act of confronting and defeating the powers.
6: Moses and Aaron did as YHWH commanded — exactly. The obedience formula ka'asher tzivah YHWH is unqualified. This complete covenant obedience mirrors Christ's perfect obedience to the Father: 'I always do the things that are pleasing to him' (John 8:29). The exodus mediators' obedience is a type of the one who was 'obedient to the point of death' (Philippians 2:8) and through whose obedience 'many will be made righteous' (Romans 5:19).
7: Moses was eighty, Aaron eighty-three when they spoke to Pharaoh. The age detail grounds the narrative historically but also points forward: the deliverer came not in the vigor of youth but in the fullness of time and preparation. The NT analogue is not age-literal but pattern-significant: Christ came 'when the fullness of time had come' (Galatians 4:4) — the timing of both deliverers was entirely determined by YHWH, not by the deliverers themselves.
8: YHWH speaks to Moses and Aaron: when Pharaoh asks for a sign, Aaron is to throw his staff down. The invitation to demonstrate divine power sets up the first confrontation between YHWH's representatives and the court magicians. This is the paradigm of every encounter between the word of God and the wisdom of the world — the world demands signs (1 Corinthians 1:22), and YHWH provides a demonstration that cannot ultimately be matched.
9: Aaron throws down his staff and it becomes a tanin — a serpent/sea-monster. The same word is used in Genesis 1:21 for the great sea creatures and in Ezekiel 29:3 for Pharaoh himself as 'the great dragon lying in the Nile.' The staff-serpent confrontation is cosmic: YHWH's rod becomes the creature that Pharaoh embodies. The one who would crush the serpent (Genesis 3:15) acts first through a sign that makes the serpent serve YHWH's purpose.
10: Aaron throws his staff before Pharaoh and his servants and it becomes a serpent — the sign performed publicly, before the highest court. The public context matters: the defeat of the serpent will not happen in private. Both the plagues and the cross are public acts — YHWH's power displayed before witnesses. 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The open display of power over the serpent begins here.
11: Pharaoh's wise men and sorcerers (chakhamim, mekashefim) replicate the sign with their secret arts. The magicians can produce serpents too — false signs that superficially match YHWH's. This is the NT's persistent warning about counterfeit signs: 'False christs and false prophets will arise and perform great signs and wonders, so as to lead astray, if possible, even the elect' (Matthew 24:24). The ability to perform signs is not in itself proof of divine authorization.
12: Aaron's staff swallowed (vayivla) all the magicians' staffs. The decisive sign: YHWH's serpent consumes the imitations. This is the first act of the plague sequence but theologically the most important: the enemy's power is not denied but absorbed and overcome. The cross follows the same logic — death is swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54, quoting Isaiah 25:8). The enemy's best weapon (the serpent, death) is swallowed by the one it tried to destroy.
13: Pharaoh's heart was hardened and he did not listen. The first hardening confirms the pattern established in Exodus 7:3. The cumulative effect of Pharaoh's refusals forms a theological argument: persistent rejection of YHWH's word hardens the heart further with each refusal. Paul uses this same logic when he describes those who suppress the truth (Romans 1:21–28) — each rejection intensifies the blindness it produces.
14: YHWH tells Moses: 'Pharaoh's heart is heavy (kaved) — he refuses to let the people go.' The kaved (heavy/hardened) heart of Pharaoh and the kavod (glory/heaviness) of YHWH share the same root — the display of YHWH's glory is directly linked to overcoming the human heart's heaviness. What glorifies YHWH most is the deliverance of people from the condition that has made them spiritually heavy and unresponsive.
15: YHWH instructs Moses to go to Pharaoh at the Nile in the morning — the Nile where Pharaoh went regularly. Pharaoh worshipped the Nile or monitored it as a divine resource; YHWH meets him at the place of his false security. This is the pattern of prophetic confrontation: the prophet meets the king at the place that represents his self-sufficiency. Christ confronts the rich young ruler at the point of his wealth (Mark 10:21), the scribes at the point of their legal expertise (Matthew 22:34–40).
16: Moses confronts Pharaoh with the covenant demand: 'Let my people go that they may serve me in the wilderness.' The liberation-for-service structure of the exodus is the pattern of redemption: deliverance is not from obligation but from wrong masters. Christ's redemption works the same way: 'You were bought with a price; do not become bondservants of men' (1 Corinthians 7:23); 'freed from sin, you have become slaves of righteousness' (Romans 6:18). Liberation from one lord is always liberation to another.
17: 'By this you shall know that I am YHWH: behold, I will strike the water of the Nile with the staff in my hand and it shall be turned to blood.' The water-to-blood sign is the first of the ten plagues. Blood in Hebrew thought marks both life (Leviticus 17:11 — 'the life of the flesh is in the blood') and death (murder, sacrifice, judgment). Turning the Nile to blood transforms Egypt's life-source into a death-marker. The Nile — Egypt's economic and religious foundation — is the first target of judgment.
18: The fish will die, the river stink, and Egypt will be unable to drink. The death of the fish strikes at Egypt's food economy; the stench marks the Nile as unclean; the inability to drink creates an existential crisis. The plague targets the whole person — sustenance, senses, survival — as divine judgment always does. The cross addresses the same totality: Christ 'bore our sins in his body' (1 Peter 2:24), becoming the comprehensive answer to comprehensive human need.
19: The command extends to all of Egypt's water — rivers, canals, ponds, pools, vessels of wood and stone. The totality of the plague is significant: every water source in Egypt, not just the Nile, turns to blood. No Egyptian could find water untouched by the judgment. This comprehensiveness prefigures the scope of Christ's work: his death and resurrection addressed not a subset of human brokenness but the total consequence of sin across every dimension of creation (Colossians 1:20 — 'reconciling to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven').
20: Moses and Aaron do as YHWH commanded — Aaron strikes the Nile with the staff, and all the water becomes blood throughout Egypt. The execution matches the command exactly. The obedience of the mediators in performing the sign is complete, as is the scope of the judgment. This is the covenant-obedience pattern that culminates in Christ's cross: 'he became obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross' (Philippians 2:8) — the supreme mediation performed with complete fidelity.
21: The fish died, the river stank, and Egypt could not drink. Three consequences, three dimensions of judgment: ecological death, sensory revulsion, physical suffering. The plague is not punishment for its own sake but a structured demonstration of what Egypt's gods could not do — they could not prevent YHWH from turning their most sacred resource against them. The Lord of all creation exercises his authority over the creation-elements that Egypt's religion had deified.
22: The Egyptian magicians did the same with their secret arts — duplicating the blood sign. But this is judgment stacked upon judgment: the magicians can turn more water to blood but cannot reverse what is already done. Their 'power' only deepens Egypt's crisis. The false prophets of the last days similarly multiply signs without healing anything; the counterfeit always intensifies the problem it pretends to solve.
23: Pharaoh turned and went home — his heart was hardened, he did not take even this to heart. 'Did not take it to heart' (lo shat libbo*) is the phrase of spiritual indifference: the sign was witnessed but not internalized. This is the parable of the sower condition (Luke 8:12) — the word heard but not absorbed. Pharaoh is the paradigm of those who see the signs of divine power and remain unmoved because the heart has grown callous (Matthew 13:15).
24: All Egypt dug around the Nile for drinking water — the plague created a national emergency. The desperation of digging beside a polluted river for filtration water depicts the human condition under judgment: improvising survival within a broken order rather than turning to the one who can restore it. The NT equivalent is the religious improvisation of those who work the law for righteousness (Romans 10:3) when the source of life is already available in Christ.
25: Seven days passed after YHWH struck the Nile. The seven-day duration of the first plague grounds it in the creation-week framework — one complete cycle of time under the blood-judgment. The seven days also establish a rhythm of warning and persistence: YHWH does not strike once and immediately destroy; there is a sustained period of consequence that allows for repentance. This is the NT principle of divine patience — 'not wishing that any should perish, but that all should reach repentance' (2 Peter 3:9).
